era_context: The early 8th century CE continues with the Umayyad Caliphate at its territorial peak, the Tang Dynasty flourishing under Emperor Xuanzong, and the Frankish kingdom consolidating power. The Byzantine Empire is experiencing increasing religious tensions that will soon erupt into the Iconoclastic Controversy. This is a period of relative stability in major civilizations, though with underlying tensions and the rise of new powers.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe and Scandinavia (sparse written records)
South Asia (limited written sources in Western archives)

events.title: Byzantine-Arab naval conflicts in the Mediterranean
events.description: The Byzantine Empire and the Umayyad Caliphate continued their long-standing naval conflicts in the Mediterranean, with both powers competing for control of trade routes and coastal territories. The Umayyads had been expanding their naval power, while the Byzantines were attempting to maintain their dominance. These conflicts were part of the broader Byzantine-Arab Wars that had been ongoing since the 7th century. Specific naval engagements for 727 are not clearly documented in surviving sources, but the general pattern of conflict is well-attested.

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: Byzantine-Arab naval conflicts in this period are well-attested, but specific events for 727 are not clearly documented. The dating is approximate based on the broader historical trajectory.
cross_references: Part of broader Byzantine-Arab Wars (7th–11th centuries)
Related to broader Mediterranean geopolitics of the early 8th century

disconfirming_evidence: No significant disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the sparse documentation for 727 CE means that any events attributed to this specific year should be understood as approximate and potentially misdated.
historiographic_note: The year 727 CE is very poorly documented in surviving sources. Most historical knowledge for this period comes from later compilations (9th–12th centuries) that synthesize earlier accounts, many of which are now lost. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward the Mediterranean world and East Asia. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, Central Asia, and South Asia are essentially absent from the written record for this specific year. Archaeological evidence is sparse and difficult to date to a single year.

graph_edges.note: Byzantine-Arab naval conflicts were part of the broader geopolitical context leading to the Battle of Tours
